title: The Suffering You Can Actually Prevent
hook: Patanjali spent one sutra — four words — on the only suffering you can actually do something about.

script.visual: Clock at 2:47 AM. Person lying awake, eyes open, replaying a cringe moment from 2019.
script.voiceover: It's 2:47 AM. You're replaying a thing you said in 2019. Stop.

script.visual: Close-up on Sanskrit text: हेयं दुःखमनागतम्. Person's finger taps under the word 'anagatam'.
script.voiceover: Sutra 2.16. Four words. Heyam duhkham anagatam. Translation: 'The suffering that has not yet come — that is the one to prevent.' Read that again. Not the past. Not the present. The future.
script.text_overlay: Heyam duhkham anagatam = prevent the suffering that has NOT YET come

script.visual: Three quick split-screens: (1) person doom-rehearsing an argument that hasn't happened, (2) person regretting a text from 2019, (3) person in current stress.
script.voiceover: Patanjali does a surgeon's triage here. Past suffering? It's done. You cannot undo the breakup, the mistake, the funeral. Present suffering? You're already in it. Endure it. But the suffering that has not yet arrived — the one your mind is rehearsing at 2 AM — that one is on the menu. That one you can stop.
script.text_overlay: Past: done / Present: endure / Future: PREVENT

script.visual: Person sits up, gets out of bed, drinks water. Simple, deliberate actions.
script.voiceover: This is not positive thinking. Patanjali is not saying 'don't worry, be happy.' He is saying: most of the suffering rattling around your head right now is suffering that has not happened. It is debt you are pre-paying. Every time you rehearse the worst-case scenario, you suffer once now and once later if it happens. Twice, for the price of one.
script.text_overlay: You suffer twice when you rehearse.

script.visual: Person back in bed, eyes closed, calm. The clock now reads 3:12 AM but they're asleep.
script.voiceover: The action he wants from you is not denial. It is this: build the practice now that makes future-you suffer less. That is all yoga is.
What future suffering are you rehearsing tonight? Name it. Then stop paying for it.

cta: What future-pain are you pre-paying for in your head right now? Write it down. Then ask if it's actually due.
caption:
Four Sanskrit words. One of the most brutal, useful sentences ever written about the mind.

Sutra 2.16: हेयं दुःखमनागतम्
heyam duhkham anagatam

"The suffering that has not yet come — that is the suffering to be prevented."

Patanjali is doing triage.

Past suffering? Done. You can't unsay the thing. Grieve it and move.
Present suffering? You are in it. Endure it.
Future suffering? That one is actually on the menu. That one you can stop.

Here is the move most of us miss: the 2 AM replay of a conversation from 2019 is NOT past suffering. It is future suffering you are manufacturing right now, because the mind keeps the wound alive. Same with the argument you are rehearsing with your boss before it happens.

You suffer twice when you rehearse. Once now. Once if it happens.

Yoga is not positive thinking. Yoga is the surgical practice of not pre-paying for pain that has not yet arrived.

What are you rehearsing tonight?

#yogasutras #patanjali #yogadarshan #anxiety #overthinking #mindfulness #meditation #suffering #3am #mentalhealth #ancientwisdom #consciousness #presence #philosophy #mindbody
music_suggestion: Start with a slow ticking clock. Soft piano enters at sutra reveal. Swells slightly at 'twice, for the price of one.' Resolves to silence + single exhale for the ending.
